speaker: interviewer
text: August 6, 1995. And you were telling us about making holes in the matzo.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this is like, you know, they make the [NON-ENGLISH] would cut it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --by hand. You used to make this by hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you used to do that, or the baker?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- everybody did it. Where ever you wanted, you help out. The baker charge you for the-- for the time, and for the oven, and for the flour, for everything. We used to go and have hot. And I was very observant. [INAUDIBLE] was very observant. We asked the questions. We asked [INAUDIBLE]. And [? father ?] explain everything. That's what was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when things began to change in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about 17 years. By 17 years old, I already saw difference. Like my father [INAUDIBLE]. I already smoked. And he didn't let me smoke. I didn't have any money. He give me $0.50 a week. And for $0.50 a week, you can-- a packet cigarette, it cost 3 and 1/2 cent a cigarette. It cost $0.70.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we try. Me and my older brother, we try and already go in the evening to help out other guys in tailoring. Because, for example, if you just marry, and you got a store-- like, you have a machine, you work by yourself-- you cannot be in two places, in store and in the shop to make it. So we go in [INAUDIBLE] to charge you $0.30. We charge you $0.25. So we said, let's see from 6:00 to 10:00, we make 10 pairs. And we got $2.50.

speaker: interviewee
text: But 5 times $2.50, you got $12.50. Comes Friday, you got $6.25. And he got $6.25. So we got something. We can move. You know, it was already difference layouts, which not only we didn't go out [INAUDIBLE]. But it come, you can go to a movie, which a movie cost $0.54 for a person to go in. But the parents didn't give us the money then, this one. They said, you [INAUDIBLE]. You got clothes on yourself. You got what to sleep. And you got what to eat. If they gave you-- if you need a ice cream or something, we gave you $0.50. That's very fine. But, you know, you desire a little bit more and more. And after you start going out with a girl already, you cannot say, you pay for your ice cream. And I pay for mine. You must pay for her ice cream, you know. It start going we are difference [INAUDIBLE]. So you demands are very more and more demanding. Tell to them, and they don't want to listen to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: They say, who told you [INAUDIBLE] girl. [? When ?] [? you ?] [? could ?] [? be ?] already? We going to see you got a girl. Because in Europe, you know, they're matchmakers. So we didn't fight too much. We just did what we could, do the best. So was going on, going on, till the war broke out. And when the war broke out, I wasn't even home. Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, I run away. I run away from the house. And I heard it. But my parents took all the stuff, [INAUDIBLE], it was a big city. So they took it, all the stuff, and hired a buggy and a horse. And they took it to his parents. And this was about 40 kilometer in a small town. After them, I come home about 10 days or 12 days after the war-- after the war started. And they are home. And it has calmed down. And they say, OK, we must go and bring some stuff back. But you cannot already hire a buggy, because they're going to rob you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we must go and bring it everything around you. You know? Take like 10 meters of material, bind around you. Put a coat on it, and go on the train, and come. And so we was going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this time, was not allowed the Jews already to walk in the night. I didn't even know this. But when I was in Krakow, and I was working in a restaurant. And the Germans was occupying this restaurant. So they give me this-- I can walk around with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a special permit?

speaker: interviewee
text: A permit, right. I got it, so I can walk around to them. And when I come and I go to the-- to the small town, and I take around it with the material, I was going. And my stepmother was going. And other brother was going. And we come home. 8 o'clock, we come to the city. And everybody goes out. And I go with them. But I didn't-- not the mother, not the brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And me, when I comes close to my house, a German stop me. He say, where are you going? Who are you? I say, I go home. I live here. He say, you got some papers? I take out this paper. And I show it to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he reads. And say, OK, go ahead. And I ring the bell.

speaker: interviewer
text: They open the door for me. And I go in. He say, how you come in? I say, what do you mean, how? I tell the story. He say, how come you go back and bring mother? I said, I don't know I can bring mother. But I can go over there, over there and take from her the material.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, no, you're not going to-- not going to go with the material. Because you can take by your brother the material. Because your mother [? you going ?] [INAUDIBLE] washroom. There will be something [INAUDIBLE]. So I was going with my brother, took [INAUDIBLE] brother's [INAUDIBLE], come home, laid down in bed like nothing happened. And after that, we suffering, suffering, suffering. They come. And they confiscated everything anyway. They give us paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: They confiscated everything. Who hold papers, confiscated paper. Who knows what they're doing? So what did they confiscate, the materials, the machines?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the finished stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the machine, only the suits, and the coats, and the pants, and the jackets. Everything, the finished [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They confiscate. They give you a paper that they took it. So what can you do? Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was already in-- in 1940 Yom Kippur, we was going to synagogue. And the past time, you know, to relax, and now [INAUDIBLE] give you relaxation. So I took my hat, you know, hide it under the jacket. And I go out. And they caught me to work. And my father said, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what's mean this? Good for him this way. Why, he's supposed to sit here, not to run away. But he worked till night. And the night, let you done-- let you go home. So time was [INAUDIBLE] it was impossible to make a living. Because the bread was so high. Potatoes you cannot get, because-- unless you going to go on the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the farm was approximately 20, 25 miles away. And that's not sure they're going give it-- sell it to you. So we decided where we and another two, three boys, let's go to the-- the Third Reich.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, stop for a minute. Now, were you are already in the ghetto at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the ghetto, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Automatically the ghetto, the big ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when your family went into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was in the same apartment we was before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. So you stayed in your apartment, and the ghetto came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. You see, the ghetto-- we was not allowed to go to the-- to the other street, like the [? intelligence ?] street, like, where the parks are. The ghetto is around you, because I just told you before that the Jews live like in a ghetto before.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a neighborhood, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in their own neighborhood, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was somebody living over there in the high places. So they must move back here. But we already lived in the same house--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the big ghetto, was it locked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No fences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, fence. And we must carry the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you want to go outside, they-- they don't recognize you, you take off the star. But if they catch you, they could kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided that we going to go to Third Reich. In the Third Reich was cost 2 marks a bread. 2 marks was equal to 4 zlotys in our money. But for us was almost 40 zloty a bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided let's smuggle it. So we was smuggling for quite a few weeks twice a week, sometimes three times a week, sometimes once a week, you know, till they caught us. And when they caught us, they put us in a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what means a [NON-ENGLISH]? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: A [NON-ENGLISH] means where they-- where they bring all together. And this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: A special camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A special-- where they-- they bring all together, like, a [INAUDIBLE], a meeting- a meeting room.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then all the-- and they took maybe it was 300 or 400. And this was Kochanovice.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Kochanovice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-oh, I told you I need a pencil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's try spelling it together.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-C-H-A-N-O-V-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So that was that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a-- that was not a camp. That was-- we didn't do nothing over there. We just-- you know, they brought-- how many they grabbed, they brought them in. And we was over 300. So they took us. And they transport us to Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell Blechhammer for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-L-A-C-H-H-A-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. And this was still in Poland, Blechhammer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it? It was into Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [INAUDIBLE] was not Poland. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what mean [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-uh.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, Poland was, once upon a time, very little. But Poland expanded himself. And they took away from Germany cities. I think in the 17-- or this one. So there was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Blechhammer, we already working. They make us working. And there was too many.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was already sleeping three, four on a bridge-- on this one, you know, three, four, maybe five. So the meanwhile, they use-- [INAUDIBLE] [? need ?] people. So I was going the first transport to Brande.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brande, B-R-A-N-D-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was by Opel. And over there, there was a camp they called Arbeitslaager. And there was an organization-- a German organization they called [INAUDIBLE], the Organization Todt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they build bridges, repaired the roads, you know? And we was behind them. On this time, we didn't have the SS. We had the [? Akka. ?] Who were the [? Akka? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans, but in uniforms. But they're not SS. They were the [? Akka. ?] And they was not strict like the SS. You can see it there was not only Jews. We were separated. Every nation was separated. But you saw the other side there were Russians, and English people working over there. But they're making bridges, you know. The Germans, they were a special organization. They hang the bridges. And they make fixing the road, what is bombed already. And we worked over there over two years. And when they finished the work, and it was already too far for us to travel with them [? farther, ?] they took us. And they transfer us to Hirschberg. And we come to Hirschberg--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year this was, or a date for when this was when you were transferred to Hirschberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirschberg, I was transferred I think the end of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are-- we are told that a-- a German delegation to the camp. And they're saying, we have nothing against you. We are paying to the SS money for you to work for us. Any sabotage you going to do, they're going to punish you. If you're going to behave yourself good, you're going to work good, we going to behave to you good. Because we paying for your work to the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they divided. Over there, they make from wood material. So we was divided-- let's see, we was about 2,000 people. We was divided 500 go to this section, 500 go to this section, you know, it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, you've got kapos with them. And you've got already [INAUDIBLE]. You know, you got this one. I fall in in the kessel house. And I work in the kessel house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was kessel house the name--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kessel house mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of a place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Kessel-- that's one place. Kessel house means an oven. Kessel is an oven.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that oven is always fire. And on top of the oven is pipes. And the pipes is always liquid something over there in the pipes. And the fire supposed to do something for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it gets clogged up. So meanwhile, they stop this oven. And they turn on the other oven. So we supposed to go in and unclog it, the pipes. Do you know what I'm talking? I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So your job was to unclog pipes--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unclog the pipes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a very hot--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hot oven. You go in [INAUDIBLE] with a mask for two minutes. At first, you go in with the-- a stick and a-- a hook. And you hit it, hit it, hit it, hit it how much you can. You run away, because you cannot breath. Next two, three go in, and you going in-- the only going in was this wide. And that's a-- that's where you can go in. But later, when it gets a little bit cooler, let's say today, and tomorrow you already go in, bring in a ladder. And you go in with hammer, hammer and chisels. And you unclog that-- this one. And I was always-- I-- I was always a good worker. Not only by the Germans, even the [? house, ?] wherever I go, I was a good worker. And food I didn't have. I always was hungry. And [INAUDIBLE] I was more hungry than anybody else, because I was stupid. Because I give away tomorrow's bread for today she give me a cigarette. I tell you the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sold tomorrow's bread, because she gave me today a cigarette. And one time, I got swollen up. All the whole thing was swollen up on me. And I couldn't walk more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't stand up and walk. So they took two canes. And they put me on the canes. And they carry me to the camp. A camp orderly was like a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like a sick room.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was over there like a doctor. He was not a doctor. He was a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put me on a bed. And somebody else took my job. But the German say, hey, how come you took away the good man, and they give me a bad man? So they come in [NON-ENGLISH] this one from the-- from the camp. And they ask the doctor, what's the matter with him? He say, show it to him. He give me [INAUDIBLE] food. He said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. You want to eat? I said, sure. He said, go in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the kitchen was outside the camp. And I barely go to the kitchen. And over there was a big, big-- David they called his name, a German. And he gave me food. I never ate so much in my life. And he say, sit here. You're going to eat. And I ate. Three days later, I was on the job. But he give an idea. He said, you got to make yourself-- there was a [INAUDIBLE], you know, a plumber. Make yourself a bucket from aluminum. And you come every day here. And we going to fill it up for you with soup. So, you know, you got kapos. And they don't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you got it, and I don't? So I say, you know what? You're going to divide it. Oh, no, I don't like it. What do you mean, you divide it? What's the matter with him? What's the matter with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're going to go in. If you're going to divide it, every day five days you're going to get one. I already give away everything. OK. It comes the time they're saying, we going to evacuate. Where we going, we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we evacuate. We was putting us on a wagon, sitting one on the other, one on the other, one on the other. And everybody got only a blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: No clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: What clothes? Oh, you got the-- the jacket and a pair of pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody sitting with this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were at Herskovits until '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirschberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hirschberg, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirschberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hirschberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, till '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we sit in the wagon. The wagon goes. And it stopped. And you know, I feel that I can't stretch my leg. OK. And the Germans were sitting around the wagon by us. It's an open wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were sitting on the seat-- I'm sitting here, and he sits here, here In all four corners. You know, I feel it that I can already lay down. Hey, what's going on here? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I look around. And I count the people on the finger. Where are they? You know what they did? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: They cut up the floor. I don't know where they got the knife or something. And they let themselves down with a train station. And they disappeared. And they covered with a blanket. And they run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we come up to Buchenwald. And we come in to Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] First of all, [INAUDIBLE] was the kapo. They ask us, who mostly hit you, punished you, did for you wrong? People say, he did it. What he did? They told him what they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took two chairs. And they said, lay down. And they say, you dance on him. And they break him down. Buchenwald was a camp from all nations. Not only Jews, all nations. He was going out to work with maybe 100 musicians playing music when we going out to work. When we coming back from work, 100 musician [INAUDIBLE]. We go straight to the movie to eat in the movie hall. Let's see we going in 100. Maybe we come out 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know why. Every day, how much we going in, less than half out. And we get this one that we're going to go to Kurvinkel. Kurvinkel was a camp which was under the ground. Uh-oh, he gave you a signal-- under the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Kurvinkel for me? Can you spell Kurvinkel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurvinkel, K-U-R-V-I-N-K-E-L

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. So they told you they were going to take you to Kurvinkel?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- no. You see, we were sleeping five in a-- a bunk. And you want to turn over, you must tell everybody you're going to turn over. Because if you turn over, you cannot.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the night, we got one-- one blanket. In the night, you didn't know. And somebody grabbed the blanket. You all without the blanket. Tomorrow, no blanket. It's cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you give the bread, and buy yourself a blanket. So why they're going to buy a blanket? The night, they're going to steal it again. So we did it. We cut it up the blanket, the middle. And we put it on the blanket. Oh, that was smart.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have a choice. And the rest [INAUDIBLE] we [? break ?] it on the-- for the shoes. But you cut it up, they're [? round. ?] [INAUDIBLE] we put [INAUDIBLE] the shoes make it. We come into Kurvinkel. It was under a hill. The breathing was only through a pipe-- no windows, no nothing, just a pipe.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we're going to stop this tape right now. And then on the next tape, we'll talk some more about Kurvinkel, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
